speaker: interviewer
text: Isabella, we were talking just before we went on to this tape about how the town crier and the newspapers were publishing different anti-Jewish laws. Could you give me some of them and how they affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came so swiftly, all those orders. It was almost impossible to keep up with the speed of it because, as I told you, whatever they learned throughout the years-- how to refine the method-- was absolutely perfect by the time they reached Hungary-- the yellow star, the wearing of a yellow star, of how many inches it has to be, how it has to be sewn on so you can't tear it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course! We all did. Everybody had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, we wore it. Other countries, evidently, wore it in the front and in the back. We had to wear ours on the left side. And my mother was busy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to buy them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we had to buy the cloth, and my mother was busy sewing the yellow stars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, all of this-- at the start of all this, I must tell you, that we lived in a fascist country, in a hateful terrible place, with anti-Semites worse than probably in Germany or, you know, probably worse, and, and then, as in Poland, incredible anti-Semitism. So Hitler had a very easy time in my country.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also because we lived in a fascist country with a controlled press, controlled radio, we knew only, only what Hitler wanted us to know. We have never heard the name of a town called Auschwitz. We never knew anything at all what happened. Hitler was winning the war, and we were supposed to root for Hitler. And we incessantly heard him on the radio, and all the anti-Semites and all Hitler lovers were shouting, heil Hitler, heil Hitler. And we grew up with that. We thought that that was normal kind of because it lasted for so many years in our young adulthood. And cause-- we simply knew nothing, only what Hitler wanted us to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, there are so many questions that are irrelevant because, for instance, having arrived in Auschwitz, and we saw a sign called Auschwitzen, and we had no idea where we were, none whatsoever. And we never heard anything about the Warsaw ghetto. We never heard anything about deportations. We never heard anything that Hitler did not want us to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Therefore, our fear when we heard his incessant ranting and ravings and the Jews, Jews, Jude, Jude, Jude, we knew that it's all against us, and we were terrified. But we were living still in our homes. Most of Europe's Jews were already decimated, and we're still living with Persian rugs in our homes. And I think it certainly did not have to occupy Hungary, but I think it was Eichmann's last hurrah, and just for good measure, just to get rid of the Jews. And he needed no excuse for anything. He, he needed-- he didn't have to march into Hungary, as far as I could tell. Nonetheless, we were faced with all of this that has-- that the rest of Europe's Jews have already gone through, and have been murdered, and experienced all that. And we knew nothing about it. And we were learning every single day. And it was affecting our lives in an insane way. No, we were not going to school, and we're not going to the store buying a dress. We were-- all we had to do is constantly comply by the onrushing orders that were shouted at us from the newspapers and the radio and the town crier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There has not been any food for a long time by then because Hitler stole everything from us. We had to stand in line for potatoes, and for flour, and for god knows what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Christians--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already used to that. They had to stand in line also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Christians too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, standing in line for a Jew meant that we had a few-- an extra measure of hatred thrown at us, you know? You [HUNGARIAN] zsido don't, don't stand in front of me. And [HUNGARIAN] zsido this, and [HUNGARIAN] zsido that. But we stood in line with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't really know. Since now that I told you that we, we only knew-- we have never heard of-- yes, we heard the following. Every once in a while-- every once in a while, somebody would show up in my kitchen-- a woman, a man-- sent by so-and-so from Czechoslovakia, from here to there, to spend a few hours in our house because my mother will make arrangements with somebody else to go to some other town where somebody else will receive them. And they were telling terrible tales of the Gestapo torturing people. But they were not telling us anything about Auschwitz. They didn't know it themselves probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these things happened on and off a number of times in my home. But then this person had left to the next designated safety place. Of course, all of the people who escaped to Hungary-- because that was the last bastion-- were eventually caught and taken to Auschwitz because that was Hungary's fate.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some of these things came to our attention. Of course, we didn't believe it. How can they do such things to people? It's impossible. You know, who could believe it? So you thought they were making up stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that it happens perhaps just to some people, to political opponents, but not to ordinary people, to women and children. It's impossible, you know. You would not believe if somebody came with stories like that to you, and you would not think that tomorrow it will happen to you. So it somehow-- we were terrified always because we heard all of this ranting and raving from Hitler. But it wasn't that immediate. It wasn't right in my kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as a matter of fact, I remember a very particular instance when a cousin of mine, my mother's brother's son, who was involved in trying to save Jews-- he eventually survived-- and he directed a woman, somebody very-- someone who was very important to aid and save. I believe she was from Czechoslovakia. And she was to come to my town to the railroad station, and then somebody was to accompany her to a town called, I believe, Debrecen, where my little twin brothers were perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Gisi? Was her name Gisi Fleischmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. And, and-- I don't remember the name. And this cousin came to my mother to make the arrangements. This cousin was a bearded Jew, but he was able to move yet. And this was before-- I believe, before Hitler marched in. And he negotiated with my mother for me that I was a teenager, I was ostensibly very presentable looking, and I could pass for a Gentile. And I would accompany this woman to the next town where somebody else would pick her up. But my orders were not to talk to anyone, and certainly not to talk to the woman. The woman would be sitting, I don't know, two rows away from me. All I had to do is keep an eye on her, and should somebody come and arrest her, then I would have to bring back the word on my way home again so that they would know in which town she was arrested so that they could try to make further arrangements. In any event, I was designated to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Accompany her?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to do this job. And I was terror-stricken, but there was no saying-- I couldn't say no. My mother knew what she was doing. She was saving a live-- a life. And of course, I was doing what my mother told me to do. Nonetheless, I was terror-stricken. And I carried out the whole mission. And we arrived into that town. And at the station, somebody came and fetched the woman. I never talked to her. I don't even remember how she looked. But I was very young, in my teens. This was long before Hitler marched in. And I completed the mission and came home with the next train. But, you see, there were occasional attempts at saving people. But they were few. And in the end, they usually did not succeed. I don't know where on earth she perished, or if she lives. I have no idea. And other than that, back again to the fact that we knew nothing, and never heard of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, never heard of Treblinka, never heard of any of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto set up in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, just weeks after Hitler marched into Hungary, where they allowed us to spend Passover at home. And still we were not suspicious of the fact that they might take us out of our homes. We spent Passover in our homes with reduced amounts of food, but we were able to bake matzos, or some people were able to bake it. And there was a regular Seder. By that time, my father had left for America. He came with the last ship in 1939-- he left in 1939-- in Hungary. And he arrived in 1940, I believe. And the sole purpose of his journey was to save his six children and his wife. And he spent his entire-- all his energies and all his time in Washington, banging on the doors, give me papers for my precious seven, for my six children and my wife! And Roosevelt in his, as we now know, in his infinite wisdom, did not give him the papers. And we perished, most of us. But that was the purpose of my father's coming to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: He thought he could bring everybody out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the reason he could come to America in the first place because he was a businessman who originally came to America to the World's Fair--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to sell Hungarian wine-- create a Hungarian wine pavilion or some such thing. And he returned in 10 days. In those days, nobody, almost nobody who came to America would return. They remained here. But he came back. And when he next went to the American Embassy to ask for another visa that he was to go back to America, they gave it to him without any question because they could trust the man. He returned. But he went to save his family. And the Roosevelt administration did dirt to him as it did to everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing in this country? How was he earning his living in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: He probably came with some funds and he had family here. He had sisters and a brother and probably tried to do some business. Eventually he, he became a-- he created a bookstore on the Lower East Side, which he brought in all of the books that could be salvaged from the European tragedy. And he was a successful businessman in selling. He went to Europe and brought back whatever he could save. And his first love was the Talmud, and now he was back in the business of Talmudic books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he stayed here all the years while this was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he stayed here all the years. And we had one or two communications from him, and then nothing. And whatever he learned about the war here no doubt made him the most devastated man in the world. And nothing he could do except keep-- as a matter of fact, he finally got the papers to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: For you children to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the six children and my mother. And he finally got the papers. But instead of giving it to him with all deliberate haste-- since he was already here and probably in the process of applying for American citizenship, and most of us were underaged, and we were legitimate-- but they just teased him, or whatever the hell they did, but they didn't give him the papers. And finally, when they did give it to him to go to America, it was-- [SIGHS] We went to Budapest, my mother and one of my sisters, Chica and I, to see the American Consul to get visas on Monday morning. And this was Sunday afternoon. And we heard that-- it was Sunday morning that Japan-- it was Pearl Harbor. And the Americans entered the war, so the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the papers were useless. And because we were-- Hungary was an ally of Hitler, and, and America entered the War on the side of the Allies. And then my poor father went back to Washington again, trying to get papers to go to Palestine. And that took another year and a half or so. And we finally got those papers, and I think they were-- they were good for toilet paper because Hitler had already marched into Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did everything to block his way from saving his family. And most of us perished. And so did he a few years after we got here. And he was a brokenhearted man. Did your mother talk about him? All the time. All the time that she-- [INAUDIBLE] that he's doing everything and that she missed him terribly because whatever the arranged marriage originally was-- certainly wasn't probably falling in love, but arranged by two families-- at this point in her life she was missing her partner with whom she had all these children. She was desperate for him. What was the ghetto like that was set up in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was set up-- you probably heard this from other people, but what the-- the system was that the people from the small villages, the hamlets, were brought into the big city, which was Kisvarda-- countless number of villagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. Some village that had only five Jews and some had 20 and were brought into this big city. Little did we know that it was all designed for the purpose of-- when it comes to the big showdown, they will not have to pluck out each Jew from every village, but they'll all be concentrated in this big city called Kisvarda. And they brought them in, and the word was always that it's only from towns or villages where the population is only so many hundreds of thousands or whatever. But it all sounded logical, sort of, you know, that it is only happening to those people. Bad as it is, they will be-- we will try to take care of them because we are safe in our own homes. Hell no. They, they brought them in, and then it was our turn. And they put them in a designated area of the ghetto, which was a small area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it homes, different houses, the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The temples and the homes of the ghetto, that was kind of the ghetto area, anyhow, where Jews lived only. And they were squeezed in there. And then, the day after Passover, they brought us in. We had to carry-- carry-- we were allowed to carry 50 kilos on our backs. And two gendarmes would come to each home, and they would march the family into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gendarme would be the Hungarian police?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gendarme-- most vicious animals on the face of the earth. And the man-- oh, it's too painful to talk about them. And, and they marched us into the ghetto, and they would put-- I think in our ghetto we had like nine people in seven feet of space, or some such thing, you know? And we quickly realized that the only thing that is most important-- even more important than eating the food that we were able to bring along-- was to keep clean-- not to have lice, not to have typhus. So we cropped our hair shorter, and we were washing all the time-- there still was water-- in order not to catch-- create typhus conditions which would threaten everybody's life. And we'd spend most of our time-- at least, that's how I remember it-- cleaning and washing and that sort of thing. And we stayed in the ghetto, unlike in other ghettos throughout Europe, just for a few weeks. And then, then came the big blast. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course we had no idea what is coming, but the word was, resettlement in the east or deportation. We didn't know what the hell it meant. And only for the duration of the war and we will work for the Germans. And it's going to be resettlement in the east. And then they marched us to the railroad station with the 50 kilos of our best clothing and best food, whatever we had left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole family, all the six children and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother. And everybody else's family. We had to take our best clothing along. Why we would have taken it because we don't know where we're going, so we want the best that will last longer. But this was only so they can confiscate upon arrival all our best food and best clothing. Which they did, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that march like? Was it during the day? Was it at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on a Monday afternoon. Unfortunately, I no longer remember. I can't remember what I had for breakfast. But unfortunately, I remember everything I should not-- everything that hurts, that is so painful. And you know, all of us are saturated with pain all our lives, whether we show it or not. Some people are surprised that I look like other people. It doesn't show, but it's inside. And Monday afternoon, they took us to the railroad station. But Sunday afternoon, for the first time, we saw the SS-- the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because up till then, we were-- all the orders were carried out by the Hungarian police and gendarmes, but now the SS took over, the Germans. And they sent into my ghetto, into my little area, a 16-year-old SS with a silver pistol, a German shepherd, a whip, and he called out the names-- we had to gather in the courtyard-- and called out the names that were originally compiled by the Judenrat, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish councils.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish council. They sent out name collectors to every single Jewish home. So there was no escape. The names were there. And, and we had to line up, and here was a group. And as he called the names, they had to go into the other group. And he called my mother's name. And just for good measure, he whipped her. And my brother wanted to tear him apart. And my sisters and I, standing next to my brother, stepped on his foot and held down his arms not to let him kill the German-- try to kill him. Because we knew, in seconds we learned, a whole lifetime of tragedy, and realized that he, he will create a pool of blood. He will slaughter my mother in front of our eyes, my brother. And he will not-- he will only create a pool of blood. He will not kill all of us, and we'll have to watch there. And then out of life [? is an ?] out of life, so let's go for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we prevented him. And he looked at us in such disbelief. He couldn't believe, I am not allowed to kill somebody who hits my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that very same brother of mine, the following morning when we were being deported, had, because he was very active in the ghetto-- he did all kinds of things that he could to help the, the Jews. He had some kind of paper where he could go outside of the ghetto and go out for a-- for a, a coffin, for instance, to bury somebody-- and he would come back. The coffin was filled with drugs. He went into the-- broke into the drug store that was Jewless, you know, a Jewish drug store. He broke in and he filled it with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Medications.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with medication and brought it back to the ghetto. He did all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was something like 18, I believe-- between 17 and 19. And he has-- had made some kind of arrangement with another three or four young guys who were not going to go to wherever we're going, and they created some kind of a hiding place. And I remember like 4:00 or 5:00 in the morning when we were gathered together for the deportation, my mother looked up and she said, oh my Kaddish-- you know what that means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I do, but tell the people who are listening.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people-- the son who will say Kaddish upon the death of the parent, and the Kaddish was a very precious member of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her only son.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was only done by men, and he was the only son. And my mother looked up and she said, oh my Kaddish. May God bless him. May he be safe wherever he is. And yes, he met at the agreed place in the ghetto where they created some kind of hiding place. And he showed up. And a few minutes later, he came back and he said, I can't go without you. I can't. I can't do it. [SOBBING] And he wound up in six concentration camps. They shot him in his attempt to escape. They shot him in the leg. He survived. And he's a wonderful man who, at the age of 70, enlisted in a doctoral program in Canada to study. Hungary and-- Hungary and American-- American and Hungarian Jews, he's getting a doctorate in. But he's always been a wonderful man. And always, if there is such a thing as my brother's keeper, he was and he is. And well, I think it's time for me to say something that is so important to me. And I wrote this, and I made this prayer to  whoever will listen to it. We are inching toward the end of our lives. We, the most tormented remnants of our age, perhaps of all ages. Help us end our journey with the knowledge that those who have neither a grave nor even ashes to prove that they were ever in our midst will live in the consciousness of human beings for all the time that they are on this planet. And I plead with you, all of you, to make sure [SOBBING] that you hand down this sacred legacy to those who come after you. Let me speak now of Auschwitz. But as I do, please bear in mind that everything I describe, I see. I hear. I smell. I am fed, yet I'm starved. I am free, yet I feel imprisoned. I am here, yet I'm in Auschwitz, standing terror-stricken in front of Dr. Joseph Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We're going to go on to our next tape and continue.
